title: Dubu Jorim Spicy Braised Tofu

seo.title: Korean Spicy Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim) Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Learn to make delicious Korean Spicy Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim) at home! This easy vegetarian recipe is packed with flavor and perfect for a healthy meal.
longTailKeywords: how to make Korean Spicy Braised Tofu
easy Korean Spicy Braised Tofu recipe
Korean Spicy Braised Tofu step by step
best Korean Spicy Braised Tofu at home
lowCompetitionKeywords: homemade Korean Spicy Braised Tofu recipe
seo.h1: Authentic Korean Spicy Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim)
h2Headings: What is Korean Spicy Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim)?
Ingredients for Korean Spicy Braised Tofu
Step-by-Step Korean Spicy Braised Tofu Instructions
Tips for the Best Dubu Jorim
seo.imageAlt: Close-up of a bowl of Korean Spicy Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim), garnished with green onions and sesame seeds.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a comforting bowl of homemade Korean Spicy Braised Tofu, a flavorful and easy vegetarian dish.

description: Dubu Jorim is a classic Korean side dish featuring pan-fried tofu simmered in a wonderfully savory, spicy, and slightly sweet sauce. This comforting vegetarian recipe is packed with umami flavor, making it a perfect accompaniment to any Korean meal or a delicious main course with rice. It is a hearty and wholesome dish that showcases the versatility of tofu.

instructions.title: Prepare and Pan-Fry Tofu
instructions.text: Drain the tofu block thoroughly and press out excess water using paper towels or a tofu press for at least 15-20 minutes. Slice the tofu into 1/2 inch thick rectangular pieces. Heat cooking oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat. Pan-fry the tofu slices for 3-4 minutes per side until golden brown and slightly crispy. Remove from pan and set aside.

instructions.title: Prepare the Braising Sauce
instructions.text: In a medium bowl, combine the soy sauce, gochugaru, minced garlic, sesame oil, sugar, mirin (if using), and water or vegetable broth. Stir well until the sugar is dissolved and all ingredients are thoroughly mixed. Taste and adjust seasoning if needed, adding more gochugaru for extra spice or sugar for more sweetness.

instructions.title: Layer Tofu and Aromatics
instructions.text: Arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer in a wide, shallow pan or a deep skillet. Sprinkle half of the chopped green onions over the tofu. You may also add thinly sliced onion or a few slices of chili pepper at this stage if desired for extra flavor.

instructions.title: Pour and Simmer the Sauce
instructions.text: Evenly pour the prepared braising sauce over the tofu slices in the pan. Ensure that the tofu is mostly submerged in the sauce. Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium-low heat. Do not let it boil vigorously.

instructions.title: Braise the Tofu
instructions.text: Once simmering, reduce the heat to low, cover the pan, and let the tofu braise for about 15-20 minutes. The tofu will absorb the flavors of the sauce and become tender. Periodically, gently spoon some of the sauce over the tofu to ensure even flavoring. Cook until the sauce has thickened slightly and reduced.

instructions.title: Garnish and Serve
instructions.text: Once the tofu is fully braised and the sauce has reduced to your desired consistency, remove the pan from the heat. Garnish generously with the remaining chopped green onions and a sprinkle of sesame seeds. Serve immediately with a bowl of warm Korean rice and other banchan (side dishes).

tips: Pressing the tofu thoroughly is crucial for its texture and ability to absorb the rich braising sauce, preventing a watery final dish.
Adjust the amount of gochugaru to your spice preference; for a milder taste, reduce it, or add more for an extra kick. You can also add a pinch of black pepper.
Dubu Jorim tastes even better the next day as the flavors meld. Store leftovers in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3-4 days.
article:
Embark on a culinary journey to Korea with Dubu Jorim, a beloved dish that perfectly encapsulates the vibrant flavors and comforting essence of Korean cuisine. This spicy braised tofu is a vegetarian powerhouse, offering a delightful combination of savory, spicy, and slightly sweet notes, all coating tender pieces of tofu. Whether you're a seasoned chef or a home cook looking to explore new flavors, Dubu Jorim is an accessible and incredibly satisfying dish that deserves a spot in your recipe repertoire. It’s a testament to how simple ingredients, when handled with care and traditional techniques, can create something truly extraordinary.

## What is Korean Spicy Braised Tofu (Dubu Jorim)?

Dubu Jorim, literally translating to "tofu simmered in sauce," is a classic Korean banchan (side dish) or a light main course, especially popular among vegetarians and those seeking plant-based protein. The "jorim" cooking technique involves simmering ingredients in a flavorful, often spicy, soy-based sauce until it reduces and beautifully coats the food. For Dubu Jorim, firm tofu slices are pan-fried (or sometimes just directly braised) and then gently simmered in a robust sauce made primarily from gochugaru (Korean chili flakes), soy sauce, garlic, and often gochujang (Korean chili paste).

The magic of Korean Spicy Braised Tofu lies in its ability to transform humble tofu into a dish bursting with flavor and texture. The tofu itself acts like a sponge, soaking up all the rich, complex flavors of the braising liquid. The slight crispness from pan-frying (if you choose that method) adds another layer of enjoyment, providing a delightful contrast to the soft interior. This dish is not just food; it’s comfort, tradition, and a celebration of simple, wholesome ingredients.

## Ingredients for Korean Spicy Braised Tofu

Creating delicious Korean Spicy Braised Tofu requires a few key ingredients, many of which are staples in a Korean pantry. The quality of these ingredients will significantly impact the final taste.

**For the Tofu:**
*   **Firm or Extra-Firm Tofu:** Approximately 14-16 ounces (one block). These types hold their shape best during braising and pan-frying. Avoid silken or soft tofu, as it will crumble.
*   **Vegetable Oil:** For pan-frying the tofu (optional, but recommended for better texture).

**For the Braising Sauce (the heart of the Jorim):**
*   **Gochugaru (Korean Chili Flakes):** 2-3 tablespoons. This provides the signature heat and vibrant red color. Adjust to your spice preference.
*   **Gochujang (Korean Chili Paste):** 1 tablespoon. Adds a deeper, fermented chili flavor, a touch of sweetness, and thickness to the sauce.
*   **Soy Sauce:** 3-4 tablespoons. The primary savory base. Use low-sodium if preferred.
*   **Garlic:** 3-4 cloves, minced. Essential aromatic.
*   **Sugar or Rice Syrup:** 1-2 teaspoons. Balances the spice and adds a touch of sweetness.
*   **Sesame Oil:** 1 teaspoon. Adds a nutty, aromatic finish.
*   **Water or Vegetable Broth:** 1/2 to 3/4 cup. The liquid for simmering.

**Optional Aromatics & Garnish:**
*   **Green Onions:** 2 stalks, sliced (for both sauce and garnish).
*   **Onion:** 1/4 small, thinly sliced (adds sweetness and depth).
*   **Toasted Sesame Seeds:** For garnish.

## Step-by-Step Korean Spicy Braised Tofu Instructions

Making Korean Spicy Braised Tofu is straightforward, but a few steps ensure the best results.

**Step 1: Prepare the Tofu**
1.  **Press the Tofu:** This is crucial! Wrap the block of tofu in paper towels or a clean kitchen towel. Place a heavy object (like a few cans or a cast-iron skillet) on top and let it press for at least 30 minutes, or up to an hour. This removes excess water, allowing the tofu to absorb the sauce better and giving it a firmer texture.
2.  **Slice the Tofu:** Once pressed, slice the tofu into rectangular pieces, about 1/2 inch thick and 2-3 inches long.
3.  **Pan-Fry (Optional but Recommended):** Heat about 2 tablespoons of vegetable oil in a non-stick skillet over medium-high heat. Fry the tofu slices for 3-4 minutes per side, until golden brown and slightly crispy. This step creates a better texture and helps the tofu hold its shape during braising. Remove and set aside.

**Step 2: Prepare the Braising Sauce**
1.  In a medium bowl, combine the gochugaru, gochujang, soy sauce, minced garlic, sugar (or rice syrup), and sesame oil.
2.  Add 1/4 cup of the water or vegetable broth to the sauce mixture and stir well until all ingredients are thoroughly combined and the sugar has dissolved.

**Step 3: Assemble and Braise**
1.  In a wide, shallow pan or skillet (the same one used for frying the tofu works well, just wipe it clean), lay down the optional sliced onions and half of the sliced green onions.
2.  Carefully arrange the pan-fried tofu slices in a single layer on top of the aromatics.
3.  Evenly pour the prepared braising sauce over the tofu.
4.  Add the remaining 1/4 to 1/2 cup of water or vegetable broth to the pan. The liquid should come about halfway up the sides of the tofu.
5.  Bring the sauce to a gentle simmer over medium heat. Once simmering, reduce the heat to medium-low, cover the pan, and let it braise for 10-15 minutes, or until the sauce has thickened and coated the tofu, and most of the liquid has been absorbed. Periodically spoon some sauce over the tofu to ensure even flavor distribution.

**Step 4: Garnish and Serve**
1.  Remove the pan from the heat.
2.  Garnish with the remaining sliced green onions and a sprinkle of toasted sesame seeds.
3.  Serve your delicious Korean Spicy Braised Tofu immediately with a bowl of warm rice.

## Tips for the Best Dubu Jorim

Achieving the perfect Korean Spicy Braised Tofu is all about attention to detail. Here are some tips to elevate your dish:

*   **Don't Skip Pressing the Tofu:** This is the most critical step. Well-pressed tofu has a better texture and absorbs flavors more effectively.
*   **Pan-Frying for Texture:** While optional, pan-frying the tofu creates a lovely golden crust and prevents the tofu from becoming mushy during braising. It adds a textural dimension that makes a big difference.
*   **Taste and Adjust the Sauce:** Before adding the sauce to the tofu, taste a small amount and adjust the spice, sweetness, and saltiness to your preference. You can add more gochugaru for heat, more sugar for sweetness, or a tiny bit more soy sauce for salt.
*   **Gentle Simmering:** Braise the tofu over a gentle simmer, not a rolling boil. This allows the tofu to slowly absorb the flavors without breaking apart.
*   **Even Coating:** During the last few minutes of simmering, periodically spoon the sauce over the tofu to ensure every piece is beautifully coated and infused with flavor.
*   **Quality Ingredients:** Use good quality gochugaru and gochujang. These are the stars of the sauce and will significantly impact the authentic Korean flavor.

## Serving Suggestions for Braised Tofu

Korean Spicy Braised Tofu is incredibly versatile and can be enjoyed in various ways.

*   **With Steamed Rice:** This is the quintessential pairing. The rice acts as a perfect canvas, soaking up the flavorful sauce and balancing the spice.
*   **As a Banchan:** Serve it alongside other Korean side dishes like kimchi, pickled radishes, and seasoned spinach for a complete Korean meal spread.
*   **In a Bibimbap Bowl:** Add slices of Dubu Jorim to your bibimbap for an extra boost of flavor and plant-based protein.
*   **Meal Prep:** It reheats beautifully, making it an excellent option for meal prepping throughout the week. Pack it in a bento box with rice and other sides.
*   **Wrap in Lettuce:** For a lighter option, wrap a piece of braised tofu with some rice and a fresh lettuce leaf for a refreshing bite.

## Variations & Substitutions for Dubu Jorim

One of the joys of cooking is the ability to customize dishes to suit your taste and dietary needs. Korean Spicy Braised Tofu is no exception.

*   **Adjusting Spice Levels:** For less heat, reduce the gochugaru and gochujang. For more heat, increase them! You can also add a pinch of black pepper.
*   **Adding Vegetables:** Elevate your Dubu Jorim by adding other quick-cooking vegetables. Sliced zucchini, mushrooms (shiitake or cremini), bell peppers, or even thin slices of potato can be added to the pan along with the tofu, allowing them to braise in the flavorful sauce.
*   **Sweetness Options:** If you don't have sugar, honey, agave nectar, or rice syrup can be used to balance the spice.
*   **Gluten-Free Version:** Easily make this dish gluten-free by substituting regular soy sauce with tamari.
*   **Oil-Free Option:** While pan-frying adds texture, you can skip this step if you prefer an oil-free dish. Simply place the pressed tofu directly into the pan with the sauce. The texture will be softer, but still delicious.
*   **Umami Boost:** For an extra layer of umami, you can add a small pinch of mushroom powder or a splash of vegetarian dashi to the braising liquid.

Dubu Jorim is more than just a dish; it's an experience. Its vibrant flavors, satisfying texture, and wholesome ingredients make it a standout in Korean cuisine. Whether you're seeking a hearty vegetarian meal or a flavorful side dish, this Korean Spicy Braised Tofu is sure to impress. Don't hesitate to dive into your kitchen and create this delightful and healthy culinary masterpiece!
